_meta.description: Comprehensive Linux Commands Database with Educational Content

find_and_delete.description: Find and delete files matching a pattern
find_and_delete.explanation: Searches current directory for .tmp files and deletes them. -type f ensures only files, not directories

grep_count_occurrences.description: Count occurrences of a pattern in files
grep_count_occurrences.explanation: grep searches recursively, wc -l counts the lines (occurrences)

list_by_size.explanation: -l (long format), -h (human readable sizes), -S (sort by size, largest first)

list_by_date.description: List files sorted by modification time
list_by_date.explanation: -t sorts by time (newest first), combine with -r for oldest first

disk_usage_sorted.description: Show disk usage sorted by size
disk_usage_sorted.explanation: du shows disk usage, sort -rh sorts reverse (-r) human-readable (-h) numbers

find_large_files.explanation: Finds large files and displays name with size in human-readable format

count_files_by_extension.command: find . -type f | sed 's/.*\.//' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
count_files_by_extension.explanation: Extracts extensions, sorts, counts unique ones, and sorts by frequency

monitor_log_file.explanation: -f follows the file, showing new lines as they're added. Press Ctrl+C to stop

search_and_replace_files.description: Search and replace text in multiple files
search_and_replace_files.explanation: Finds .txt files and replaces old with new in-place. Remove -i to preview first

top_processes_by_memory.description: Show top 10 processes using most memory
top_processes_by_memory.explanation: Lists processes sorted by memory usage, head shows top 10 (11 includes header)

top_processes_by_cpu.description: Show top 10 processes using most CPU
top_processes_by_cpu.explanation: Lists processes sorted by CPU usage (descending)

check_port_usage.description: Check what's using a specific port
check_port_usage.explanation: Shows process using port 8080. May need sudo for all processes

archive_directory.explanation: -c (create), -z (gzip), -f (file). Use -j for bzip2, -J for xz compression

find_recently_modified.description: Find files modified in last 24 hours
find_recently_modified.explanation: -mtime -1 means less than 1 day ago. Use -mmin -60 for last 60 minutes

disk_space_by_directory.description: Show disk usage for each directory
disk_space_by_directory.explanation: Shows size of immediate subdirectories, sorted largest first

count_lines_in_files.description: Count total lines across all files
count_lines_in_files.explanation: Counts lines in all Python files, tail shows total

kill_processes_by_name.description: Kill all processes matching a name
kill_processes_by_name.explanation: -f matches full command line. Use killall for exact name match
kill_processes_by_name.safer_alternative: pgrep -f 'process_name' (to see what would be killed first)

watch_command_output.description: Run command repeatedly and watch output
watch_command_output.explanation: Runs df -h every 2 seconds. Press Ctrl+C to stop

find_duplicate_files.explanation: Calculates MD5 hash of each file, finds duplicates by hash

find_empty_files.explanation: Finds both empty files (0 bytes) and empty directories

parallel_compression.explanation: Uses pigz (parallel gzip) for faster compression on multi-core systems

batch_rename_files.command: for f in *.txt; do mv "$f" "${f%.txt}.md"; done
batch_rename_files.explanation: Changes all .txt files to .md. ${f%.txt} removes .txt extension

find_and_grep.explanation: Finds .log files containing 'ERROR'. -l shows only filenames

monitor_directory_changes.description: Watch for file changes in directory

network_bandwidth_test.explanation: Measures TCP/UDP bandwidth. Run 'iperf3 -s' on server first

csv_column_extraction.explanation: Extracts columns 1 and 3. -d sets delimiter, -f selects fields

json_pretty_print.explanation: Formats JSON with proper indentation for readability

ls.description: List directory contents - one of the most fundamental commands

common_options.-l: Long format with permissions, owner, size, date
common_options.-a: Show all files including hidden (starting with .)
common_options.-t: Sort by modification time, newest first
common_options.-S: Sort by file size, largest first

examples.explanation: Long format showing all files including hidden
Long format, human-readable sizes, sorted by size

tips: Combine multiple options: ls -lAhtr shows all files, long format, human sizes, sorted by time (oldest first)
Use ls -d */ to list only directories
ls -1 shows one file per line (useful for scripting)

examples.explanation: Go to Documents in home directory

tips: Use 'pushd' and 'popd' for directory stack navigation
'cd' with no arguments goes to home directory
Use Tab completion to avoid typing full paths

pwd.description: Print working directory - show current location
tips: Useful in scripts to save current location: OLDPWD=$(pwd)
Combine with command substitution: cd $(dirname $(pwd))

common_options.-s: Squeeze multiple blank lines into one
common_options.-E: Display $ at end of each line

examples.explanation: Create new file (type content, Ctrl+D to save)

tips: For large files, use 'less' or 'more' instead
Use 'tac' to display file in reverse order
cat without arguments reads from stdin (useful in pipes)

grep.description: Search text using patterns - powerful text search tool

common_options.-C NUM: Show NUM lines before and after
common_options.-o: Show only matched part of line

examples.explanation: Recursively search for 'TODO' with line numbers
Show lines not starting with # (exclude comments)

tips: Use grep -P for Perl-compatible regex
Combine with other commands: ps aux | grep nginx
Use grep -c to count occurrences instead of piping to wc -l

find.description: Search for files and directories in hierarchy

examples.explanation: Find all .txt files in current directory and subdirectories
Find files modified in last 7 days
Find large files and show their sizes

tips: Use -maxdepth N to limit search depth
Combine multiple conditions: find . -name '*.txt' -size +1M
Use -print0 with xargs -0 for filenames with spaces

awk.description: Pattern scanning and text processing language

examples.explanation: Print 1st and 3rd column from each line
Show PID and command name from ps output

tips: $0 is the entire line, $1 is first field, $2 second field, etc.
Use BEGIN and END blocks for initialization and summary
Built-in variables: NR (line number), NF (number of fields)

sed.description: Stream editor for filtering and transforming text
tips: Test without -i first to preview changes
Use sed -i.bak to create backup before modifying
Combine multiple commands: sed -e 's/a/b/' -e 's/c/d/'

ps.description: Report process status - show running processes

examples.explanation: Show all running processes with detailed info

tips: Use 'top' or 'htop' for real-time monitoring
ps aux shows: USER, PID, %CPU, %MEM, COMMAND
Combine with pgrep for specific searches

top.description: Display and update sorted information about processes
tips: Press 'M' to sort by memory, 'P' for CPU
Use 'htop' for a more user-friendly interface
top shows load average in first line: 1min, 5min, 15min

df.description: Report file system disk space usage

examples.explanation: Show disk space in human-readable format
Check space for specific mount point

tips: Use 'du' to see disk usage by directory
If df shows 100% but files deleted, check for open file handles with lsof
Watch out for inode exhaustion (df -i)

du.description: Estimate file space usage - disk usage by directory

examples.explanation: Show size of each item in current directory
Show directory sizes sorted by size

tips: du counts actual disk usage (considers hard links)
For fast estimates, use --apparent-size
Use ncdu for interactive disk usage explorer

free.description: Display amount of free and used memory in the system

examples.explanation: Show memory usage in human-readable format
Show memory in MB, update every 2 seconds

tips: 'available' column shows memory available for new apps
Buffers/cache is memory used for system caching (reclaimable)
Don't worry if 'free' is low - Linux uses free RAM for caching

tar.description: Archive utility - create and extract tar archives
tips: Remember: tar -czf creates, tar -xzf extracts
Modern tar auto-detects compression: tar -xf works for any format
Use -v to see progress during operations

wget.description: Download files from the web - non-interactive downloader
tips: Use -c to resume downloads if connection drops
wget keeps retrying if network is unstable
For single file downloads, curl is more flexible

curl.description: Transfer data from or to a server - versatile data transfer tool
tips: Use -L to follow redirects (important for many sites)
curl is better for APIs, wget for downloading files
Add | jq for pretty JSON formatting

chmod.description: Change file permissions - control who can read/write/execute

examples.explanation: Owner: rwx, Group: r-x, Others: r-x
Add execute for owner, remove write for group

tips: 755 for scripts/executables, 644 for regular files
Never use 777 unless absolutely necessary (security risk)
Use chmod -R carefully on directories
Usually requires sudo/root privileges
Use chgrp to change group only
Check current ownership with ls -l

ln.description: Make links between files - create shortcuts

link_types.hard: Direct reference to file data (same inode)

common_options.-n: Treat symlink to directory as file

tips: Always use absolute paths with symlinks
Symbolic links can point to non-existent files (check with ls -l)
Hard links can't cross filesystems or link to directories

netstat.description: Network statistics - show network connections, routing tables

examples.explanation: Show all connections with program names

tips: Use ss instead of netstat (faster, more info)
netstat -tulnp | grep :80 to check specific port
May need sudo to see all process names

ss.description: Socket statistics - modern replacement for netstat
tips: ss is faster than netstat for large systems
Use ss -o to show timer information
ss can filter by state, address, port

ping.description: Send ICMP ECHO_REQUEST to network hosts - test connectivity
tips: Press Ctrl+C to stop continuous ping
Low ping = good, high ping or packet loss = network issues
Use ping6 for IPv6 addresses

traceroute.description: Trace route to network host - show path packets take
tips: Each line shows a hop in the route
*** means that hop doesn't respond
Use mtr for continuous traceroute monitoring

kill.description: Send signal to process - terminate or control processes
tips: Always try kill (TERM) before kill -9
Use pkill to kill by name: pkill firefox
Get PID with ps, pgrep, or top

systemctl.description: Control systemd system and service manager

examples.explanation: Enable SSH to start on boot

tips: Most commands require sudo
Use systemctl daemon-reload after editing unit files
View logs with journalctl -u service-name
Use --no-pager for scripts
journalctl --disk-usage shows space used by logs
Navigate with arrow keys, space, and q to quit

cron.description: Time-based job scheduler - run tasks automatically
field_meaning: day of week (0-7, 0 and 7 are Sunday)

examples.explanation: Edit cron jobs for current user
Run backup script daily at 2 AM

tips: Use full paths in cron jobs (PATH may differ)
Redirect output: command >> /var/log/cron.log 2>&1
Test your command manually before adding to cron

lsblk.description: List information about block devices - shows disks and partitions

common_options.-a: Show all devices including empty ones

examples.explanation: List all block devices in tree format
Show filesystems, UUIDs, and mount points

tips: Use lsblk -f to see filesystem types and UUIDs
Combine with grep: lsblk | grep disk
Shows device hierarchy (disk → partitions)

fdisk.description: Partition table manipulator for Linux - manage disk partitions
tips: Always use 'sudo' with fdisk
Use 'p' in interactive mode to print partition table
Use 'w' to write changes (careful!), 'q' to quit without saving
For GPT partitions, use 'gdisk' or 'parted' instead

iostat.description: Report CPU and I/O statistics - monitor disk performance

examples.explanation: Show CPU and device statistics once
Extended stats, update every 1 second, 5 times
Device stats in KB, update every 2 seconds

tips: High %util indicates disk bottleneck
Watch await (average wait time) for slow disks
Part of sysstat package - may need to install

lsof.description: List open files - shows which processes have which files open

common_options.+D DIR: Show processes using files in directory

examples.explanation: Show all files opened by user

tips: May require sudo for full results
Use lsof -i to debug 'address already in use' errors
Combine with grep: lsof -i | grep LISTEN

dmesg.description: Display kernel ring buffer messages - system boot and hardware logs

examples.explanation: Follow new kernel messages in real-time

tips: Useful for debugging hardware issues
Check after system boot for problems
Look for USB device info: dmesg | grep -i usb

vmstat.description: Report virtual memory statistics - system performance overview
tips: High 'si' and 'so' indicate swapping (bad for performance)
Watch 'r' column for CPU queue length
First line shows averages since boot

blkid.description: Locate and print block device attributes - UUIDs, filesystems
tips: Use for finding UUIDs for /etc/fstab
May require sudo for some devices
Shows filesystem type and labels

parted.description: Partition editor - create, resize, and manage partitions
tips: Supports both MBR and GPT partition tables
Can resize partitions (use carefully!)
Always requires root privileges

mkfs.description: Make filesystem - format partitions with specific filesystem
tips: ⚠️ DESTROYS ALL DATA on the partition!
Always double-check device name
Use lsblk to identify correct device first
Changes are temporary unless added to /etc/sysctl.conf
Common tuning: net.ipv4.ip_forward, vm.swappiness
Reload config: sysctl -p
